title: Local Inference
lesson:
Local inference means running a language model on hardware you control — your laptop, your desktop, a server in your office — instead of sending your text over the internet to a company like OpenAI. "Inference" is just the fancy word for using a model that is already trained: you feed it a prompt, it predicts the answer. Doing that locally means the whole conversation happens inside your own machine and never leaves the building.

The home kitchen analogy

Think of a cloud AI API like ordering from a restaurant. You text your order, a professional kitchen with huge ovens cooks it, and the meal arrives. It is easy and the equipment is world-class, but you pay per dish, you wait for delivery, and the restaurant sees everything you ordered.

Local inference is cooking at home. You buy the ingredients once (download the model), you use your own stove (your GPU or CPU), and nobody else ever knows what you ate. The catch: your home stove is smaller than the restaurant's, so you cook simpler meals — a smaller model — and you do the dishes yourself.

Why bother running models yourself?

  • Privacy. Medical notes, legal contracts, source code, personal journals — local inference means sensitive text is never transmitted to a third party. For some businesses this is the difference between "allowed" and "illegal."
  • Cost. Cloud APIs charge per token (the chunks of text a model reads and writes). Run something a million times and the bill stings. Local inference is roughly free after the hardware — you already pay the electricity bill.
  • Offline and reliable. No internet, no rate limits, no surprise price changes, no "the API is down today." A model on your disk just works.
  • Control. You can run a fine-tuned model, an uncensored one, or an old version forever. The cloud can quietly swap or retire models under you.

The hard part: it has to fit

Here is the wall everyone hits. A model is a giant pile of numbers called parameters (the model's learned settings). To run fast, those numbers must sit in fast memory — VRAM on a graphics card, or system RAM.

A rough rule: each billion parameters needs about 2 GB of memory when quantized to 4 bits. Quantization means storing each number with fewer bits to save space — like compressing a photo. So a 7-billion-parameter model needs roughly 5 GB and runs happily on a modern laptop; a 70B model needs roughly 40 GB and demands a serious GPU or an Apple Silicon Mac with lots of unified memory.

⚠️ If a model does not fit in memory, it "spills" onto your slow disk and crawls — think of a chef who keeps running to the basement freezer for each ingredient instead of having them on the counter. Match the model size to your memory before you download 40 GB.

How you actually do it

You almost never write the math yourself. You use a runtime — an engine that loads the model and runs the prediction loop. The friendliest is Ollama, which wraps the llama.cpp engine and uses GGUF files (a single-file model format built for laptops). One command and you are chatting:

# Pull a small model and run it entirely on your machine
ollama run llama3.2:3b

Because Ollama also runs a tiny local web server, your own code can talk to it exactly like a cloud API — but the request never leaves localhost (your own computer):

import requests

r = requests.post("http://localhost:11434/api/generate", json={
    "model": "llama3.2:3b",
    "prompt": "Summarise this contract clause in one line.",
    "stream": False,
})
print(r.json()["response"])  # answer computed on YOUR hardware

The honest trade-offs

Local is not free magic. A second analogy helps: a giant cloud model is like a concert-hall orchestra; a local 8B model is like a skilled busker. The busker is right there, costs nothing, and plays your favourite tune on demand — but cannot match the orchestra's range.

Frontier-quality answers, huge context windows, and the very newest models still mostly live in the cloud. Choosing local is a deliberate trade-off of raw quality for privacy, cost, and control.

Local inferenceCloud API
Data never leaves your machineText sent to a third party
Free after hardwarePay per token, forever
Limited by your memory/GPUAccess to the biggest models
Works offlineNeeds internet, can rate-limit
You manage updates yourselfAlways patched and maintained

For one user on one device, local inference is often the smarter choice. Serving thousands of people at once is a different game — that needs a heavy server like vLLM and falls under API serving.
takeaways: Local inference runs the model on your own hardware — text never leaves the machine.
Free after hardware and works offline, but limited by your memory.
Rough fit rule: ~2 GB of memory per billion parameters at 4-bit.

diagram.caption: Local inference keeps the whole loop on your machine; the cloud path sends your text to a third party and bills per token.

quiz.q: What does running a model with "local inference" actually mean?
options: Training a brand-new model from scratch on your laptop
Using an already-trained model on hardware you own, so prompts stay on your machine
Calling a cloud API but caching the answers locally
Downloading answers in advance and replaying them offline
quiz.explain: Inference is using a trained model to get answers; doing it locally means the prediction runs on your own GPU/CPU and the data never leaves the machine. Training is a separate, far heavier process.

quiz.q: You have a laptop with 8 GB of free memory. Using the ~2 GB per billion parameters (4-bit) rule, which model is the safest fit?
quiz.explain: A 3B model needs roughly 5 GB, leaving headroom inside 8 GB. The others exceed the budget and would spill onto slow disk or fail to load.

quiz.q: A friend says "local inference is just slow" after a 70B model crawled on their laptop. What is the real problem?
options: Local runtimes are always slower than the cloud, no matter the model
The model was too big for their memory, so it spilled onto slow disk
Quantization makes every local model unusable
quiz.explain: When a model does not fit in fast memory it spills onto disk and crawls. Picking a model that fits — not blaming the tool — is the fix; a right-sized model runs fast locally.

quiz.q: Your startup needs to serve thousands of simultaneous users. Why is a single Ollama instance on one machine the wrong tool?
options: A single-machine runtime answers roughly one request at a time and stalls under concurrent traffic
Local models are illegal to use in production
Ollama requires an internet connection to serve requests
quiz.explain: Single-device runtimes are built for one user; under heavy concurrent load they bottleneck. High-throughput serving needs a stack like vLLM designed for batching many requests.

mentalModel: Local inference is cooking at home instead of ordering takeout: you own the ingredients (the model) and the stove (your GPU/CPU), so nothing leaves the house and there's no per-dish bill — but your kitchen is smaller than the restaurant's, so you cook with smaller models and do the dishes yourself.
mistakes: Downloading a model bigger than your memory. A 70B model will not fit in a laptop's RAM. It either refuses to load or spills onto disk and runs at a crawl. Check the rough rule (~2 GB per billion parameters at 4-bit quantization) before you download tens of gigabytes.

Expecting cloud-level quality from a tiny local model. A 3B model on your laptop is genuinely useful, but it is not GPT-class. Match expectations to size: for hard reasoning or huge context, the frontier models still mostly live in the cloud.

Confusing the runtime with the model. Ollama, llama.cpp, and MLX are engines; llama3.2 or qwen are the brains they run. "Local inference is slow" usually means the chosen model is too big for your hardware, not that the tool is bad.

Assuming a local server is production-grade for many users. A single-machine runtime answers requests roughly one at a time. Under real concurrent traffic it stalls. For many simultaneous users you need vLLM or another serving stack, not a laptop.

Ignoring the context-window default. Local runtimes often cap the context window (how much text the model reads at once) lower than the model actually supports, silently chopping long documents. Set it explicitly when feeding big inputs.

Forgetting that 'free' still costs power and time. Local inference uses your electricity, your battery, and your machine's full attention while it runs. On a laptop it can drain the battery fast and make everything else lag — plan for it, especially for big batch jobs.
exercise:
Pick a model that comfortably fits your machine (start with a 3B model if you have a typical laptop). Install Ollama, run it, and chat with it once to confirm it works. Then unplug your internet and ask it three more questions — prove to yourself the answers are still coming, computed entirely on your hardware with no connection. Finally, open a second terminal and hit the local API with the Python snippet from the lesson, asking the same question you asked in chat.

Stretch goal: Take a real document you would never paste into a cloud chatbot (a contract, a private note, your own code) and run a summary locally over it. Then estimate what that same job would have cost as cloud API tokens if you ran it 10,000 times, and write down the break-even point where buying a GPU becomes cheaper than paying per token.
solution: Hint: If chat works but the unplugged questions fail, the model was probably still downloading — pull it fully before going offline (ollama pull llama3.2:3b). The chat REPL and the API call the same engine, so answers should match in style (exact wording varies because generation is random unless you fix the seed). For the cost estimate: count the tokens in your document and its summary, multiply by 10,000, and compare against a cloud price of roughly a few dollars per million tokens. The break-even point is where (GPU + electricity cost) ÷ (cloud cost per job) tells you how many jobs you must run before owning hardware wins — for high-volume, repetitive, private work, local usually pulls ahead surprisingly fast.
summary: Local inference is running a trained model on hardware you own, so your data never leaves the machine, you pay no per-token bill, and you work offline with full control. The price is fit: a model must squeeze into your memory (~2 GB per billion parameters at 4-bit), so you run smaller models than the cloud's giants. Use a friendly runtime like Ollama for one user on one device; reach for vLLM when you need to serve many people at once.

keyTerms.def: Running an already-trained language model on your own hardware (laptop, desktop, office server) so prompts and answers stay on your machine instead of being sent to a cloud provider.
Using a trained model to produce an answer — feeding it a prompt and getting a prediction back. Contrasted with training, which is the slow process of teaching the model in the first place.
The program that loads a model into memory and runs the prediction loop for you, such as Ollama, llama.cpp, or MLX. You use it instead of writing the math yourself.
The fast memory on a graphics card (GPU). Models must mostly fit here to run quickly; system RAM is the fallback and is slower.
Storing each model number with fewer bits to shrink memory use, like compressing a photo. At 4-bit precision a model needs roughly 2 GB of memory per billion parameters.
A single-file model format designed for running models on regular computers. It is what llama.cpp and Ollama load, packaging the weights and settings together.
The name your computer uses to refer to itself. A local API server (e.g. at localhost:11434) is reachable only from your own machine, so requests never touch the internet.

A small chunk of text — often a word or word-piece — that a model reads and writes. Cloud APIs bill per token; local inference does not, which is why heavy, repeated use favours running models yourself.
